title: SEGMENT 1 — Why model organisms, and what they bought us

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — Why model organisms, and what they bought us
Alright, settle in.

For the next two hours we are going to talk about model organisms — and specifically about three of them: a tiny transparent worm called Caenorhabditis elegans, a fruit fly called Drosophila melanogaster, and a small striped fish called Danio rerio, the zebrafish.

These three are not the only model organisms that biology uses — there's yeast, there's E. coli, there's the mouse, there's Arabidopsis for plants — but these three cover an extraordinary amount of what we know about animal genetics, animal development, and animal nervous systems.

And if you walk out of here remembering nothing else, I want you to walk out remembering this: modern genetics rests on a small set of model organisms, each chosen for the questions it answers best, and the choice was never accidental — it was a deliberate evolutionary strategy by the field itself, picking animals whose biology matched the technical questions of the era.
Let me start with the analogy I'm going to come back to over and over again today, because if you internalize it, half the lecture becomes intuitive.
The model organisms of biology are a small-animal Olympic team.
Each one chosen for the questions it answers best.
The worm runs the cell-lineage event — because it has 959 somatic cells and you can see every one of them.
The fly runs the classical genetics event — because it has chromosomes you can map and a century of accumulated mutant strains.
The zebrafish runs the vertebrate-development event — because the embryo is transparent and a single pair of fish lays two hundred eggs in a morning.
None of them is the best athlete overall.
But each one is unbeatable in its event.
And modern biology is what you get when you put the team together and let each member compete in the discipline they were built for.
Now let me back up and ask: why model organisms at all?
Why don't we just study humans directly?
Or, if we're being more humane, why don't we just pick one animal and study only that one?
The answer is a list of practical constraints, every one of which the field figured out by painful trial and error over the twentieth century.
Let me run through them, because every one of these constraints is a reason somebody chose somebody chose worm, or fly, or fish, in the first place.
One: generation time.
Genetics is a science of crosses.
You take two animals with different genotypes, you mate them, you look at the offspring, you mate those offspring, you look at the next generation, and so on.
The faster the organism reproduces, the faster you do science.
A human takes twenty-five years per generation.
A mouse takes ten weeks.
A zebrafish takes three months.
A fruit fly takes ten days.
A worm takes three days.
You can do, in worm, ten generations in the time it takes a mouse to do one.
Ten generations is the difference between answering a question and not answering it, between a PhD thesis and a permanent mystery.
Generation time is everything.
Two: ease of genetics.
Some organisms are wildly easier to manipulate genetically than others.
Some have small genomes, simple chromosomes, well-defined developmental stages where you can inject DNA.
Others have huge complicated polyploid genomes and no obvious place to grab the embryo.
Worms and flies have famously tractable genetics.
Zebrafish have just enough tractable genetics to make vertebrate development possible at scale.

Humans, by contrast, are essentially genetically intractable — you can't cross humans on demand, you can't knock out human genes in living humans, you have to take whatever variation nature happens to provide.
The model organisms gave us the experimental dial that humans took away.
Three: conservation of biology.
The deep secret of comparative biology is that almost everything that matters at the molecular level is conserved across enormous evolutionary distances.
The gene that controls eye development in a fly — eyeless — is the same gene that controls eye development in a mouse, called Pax6.
Knock it out in either organism and you lose eyes.
The Hox genes that pattern the body plan of a fly are the same Hox genes that pattern the body plan of a vertebrate, in the same order along the chromosome, doing the same job along the body axis.
The insulin signaling pathway that controls metabolism in a worm is the same pathway that controls metabolism in a human.
We are, deep down, all running variations on the same biochemical operating system.
So a discovery made in a fly often translates to mice, and a discovery made in mice often translates to humans.
Not always.
But often enough.
That's the bet the entire model organism enterprise is built on, and over fifty years it has paid off enough times to keep being made.
Four: ethics and cost.
A worm experiment costs essentially nothing.
The animals live on a petri dish, eat bacteria, and reproduce in three days.
You don't need ethics committee approval to kill a worm.
A fly experiment is slightly more expensive but still tiny.

A fish experiment is more substantial — you need a fish facility with circulating water and temperature control — but still vastly cheaper than a mouse facility, and the ethical framework is more permissive because fish are far enough from humans that the regulatory regime is lighter.
A mouse experiment costs ten or twenty times what a fish experiment costs.
A monkey experiment costs ten or twenty times what a mouse experiment costs.

The model organism scale lets you do enormous experiments — hundreds of thousands of animals, genome-wide screens, decades-long aging studies — that you simply could not afford in a more expensive organism.
So those are the constraints.
Now the strategic question.
If you had to pick a small handful of organisms to do most of animal biology with, which would you pick?
The historical answer — and it's not random, it's a series of deliberate choices made by specific people in specific decades — converged on these three:
The worm, for cell lineage, aging, and the simplest neuroscience.
Three hundred and two neurons.
Completely mapped.
Transparent.
Three-day generation time.
The simplest animal you can imagine that still has differentiation, behavior, and learning.
The fly, for classical genetics, developmental patterning, and complex behavior.
Chromosomes you can see in the salivary glands.
A hundred years of accumulated mutant lines.
A nervous system with about a hundred thousand neurons that nonetheless supports learning, memory, sleep, courtship, circadian rhythm — the full repertoire of brain function in miniature.
The fish, for vertebrate development at scale.
Same vertebrate body plan as humans — same notochord, same neural tube, same somites, same fins where our limbs would be.
Transparent embryos so you can watch every cell of development under a microscope.
Two hundred eggs per pair per week.
The vertebrate that scaled.
These three are not competitors.
They are complements.

They cover, between them, the questions you can ask in a small animal — from gene function to neural circuitry to vertebrate organogenesis — and for fifty years they have been the primary substrate on which the genetic logic of animal biology has been worked out.
So here is the contract for the next two hours.
By the end, you should be able to do three things.
One: explain, with the Olympic team analogy, why we use a portfolio of model organisms rather than a single one, and why each of these three was chosen for the work it does.

Two: walk through, in rough terms, the major discoveries that came out of each — Sulston's cell lineage, Brenner's worm, Morgan's chromosomes, Nüsslein-Volhard's screen, Streisinger's fish, the Boston-Tübingen big screen, fish CRISPR — and have a feel for which discoveries came from which organism and why.

Three: look at a paper, a press release, or a news article about a new genetic finding, a new developmental gene, a new disease model, and have real intuition for how the underlying model-organism work led to it.
Because almost every modern genetic story, somewhere upstream, has a worm or a fly or a fish in its history.
Let's start where the worm started — with a single scientist in 1965 making a deliberate decision to bet his career on a nematode that fits on a fingernail.
